title: Podman Machine Date gets behind after sleep
body:
> This is fixed in Podman v4.5.0. You also need to re-create your podman machine with podman 4.5.0 in order for the fix to take effect. Feel free to re-open this issue if the time is sitll not synced after upgrading your version.  

 _Originally posted by @ashley-cui in [#18552](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/18552#issuecomment-1547864633)_

This still happens to me regularly (but seems to have just started after one of the last few upgrades):

```
~ podman version
Client:        Podman Engine
Version:       5.6.1
API Version:   5.6.1
Go Version:    go1.25.1
Git Commit:    1e2b2315150b2ffa0971596fb5da8cd83f3ce0e1
Built:         Thu Sep  4 15:46:52 2025
Build Origin:  pkginstaller
OS/Arch:       darwin/arm64

Server:       Podman Engine
Version:      5.2.4
API Version:  5.2.4
Go Version:   go1.22.7
Built:        Sun Oct  6 19:00:00 2024
OS/Arch:      linux/arm64
~
```

comments.body: Hi! Could you provide some more information, maybe a reproducer, or some more detail on how long it takes the machine to desync? Also, it looks like your server is much older than the client, does this still happen with a new podman machine?

Could you please use these commands to check if the NTP service has stopped working?

```
podman machine ssh systemctl status chronyd

podman machine ssh timedatectl status
```

A reviewer has determined we need more information to understand the reported issue.  A comment on what is missing should be provided. Be certain you:

* provide an exact reproducer where possible
* verify you have provided all relevant information - minimum is `podman info`
* answer any follow up questions

If no response to the `needs-info` is provided in 30 days, this issue may be closed by our stale bot.

For more information on reporting issues on this repository, consult our [issue guide](ISSUE.md).
somewhat related https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/26185

> Hi! Could you provide some more information, maybe a reproducer, or some more detail on how long it takes the machine to desync? Also, it looks like your server is much older than the client, does this still happen with a new podman machine?

It seems like it's off whenever my machine goes to sleep and it starts back up. I usually don't notice it until my machine has been off for a day or two, and then it's obvious (due to failing tests in my codebase due to the date being off in psql that is running in a docker container). If I restart or recreate my podman machine, it will temporarily fix it, but it comes back.

```

Any other ideas?

If I restart my podman VM I can get it back to normal...

```
~ podman machine stop && podman machine start

Machine "podman-machine-default" stopped successfully

Docker API clients default to this address. You do not need to set DOCKER_HOST.

                Time zone: America/Chicago (CDT, -0500)

From the logs, it looks like `chronyd` is having trouble reaching the time server. Was your host machine asleep, disconnected from the internet for a while, or did it have a slow connection? @podollb 

You can run this command to force a time sync without a restart: `podman machine ssh sudo chronyc -a makestep`.

Do `applehv` or `libkrun` have an option to 'Sync time with host'? @jakecorrenti

> Do applehv or libkrun have an option to 'Sync time with host'? @jakecorrenti 

I think that is what @ninja-quokka was looking into as part of https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/26185 / https://github.com/containers/podman-machine-os/pull/175, not sure if he has made progress on that since?

I didn't make any further progress on it. Vfkit supports timesync, krunkit doesn't. 

Next steps where to research the other hypervisors we use to see if there was a common way we could implement this. 

The crux of the issue is we should be using the host as the time source, not an external NTP server.

Due to the daemonless nature of Podman I could only image this working with support from the hypervisors.

> From the logs, it looks like `chronyd` is having trouble reaching the time server. Was your host machine asleep, disconnected from the internet for a while, or did it have a slow connection? [@podollb](https://github.com/podollb)
> 
> You can run this command to force a time sync without a restart: `podman machine ssh sudo chronyc -a makestep`.
> 
> Do `applehv` or `libkrun` have an option to 'Sync time with host'? [@jakecorrenti](https://github.com/jakecorrenti)

My machine was asleep, and then wakes up, when this occurs. From what I can tell, any time that it is asleep it loses time (i.e. never sync's up once it wakes up).

As of today's problem, after waking up the machine:

Date on host:
```
~ date
Tue Oct 21 12:30:50 CDT 2025
```

Date on VM:
```
~ podman machine ssh date
Mon Oct 20 15:56:50 CDT 2025
```

More info:
```
~ podman machine ssh timedatectl show | grep NTPSynchronized

NTPSynchronized=yes
```

I didn't notice this (from the above logs) until now:
```
Can't synchronise: no selectable sources (0 unreachable sources)
Root distance ... exceeds maxdistance of 3.000 seconds
Jitter ... exceeds maxjitter of 1.000 seconds
```

These show that chronyd is rejecting all its NTP sources because it thinks their offset or jitter is too large.

From the config:
```
~ podman machine ssh sudo cat /etc/chrony.conf

# Use public servers from the pool.ntp.org project.
# Please consider joining the pool (https://www.pool.ntp.org/join.html).
pool 2.fedora.pool.ntp.org iburst

# Use NTP servers from DHCP.
sourcedir /run/chrony-dhcp

# Record the rate at which the system clock gains/losses time.
driftfile /var/lib/chrony/drift

# Allow the system clock to be stepped in the first three updates
# if its offset is larger than 1 second.
makestep 1.0 3

# Enable kernel synchronization of the real-time clock (RTC).
rtcsync

# Enable hardware timestamping on all interfaces that support it.
#hwtimestamp *

# Increase the minimum number of selectable sources required to adjust
# the system clock.
#minsources 2

# Allow NTP client access from local network.
#allow 192.168.0.0/16

# Serve time even if not synchronized to a time source.
#local stratum 10

# Require authentication (nts or key option) for all NTP sources.
#authselectmode require

# Specify file containing keys for NTP authentication.
#keyfile /etc/chrony.keys

# Save NTS keys and cookies.
ntsdumpdir /var/lib/chrony

# Insert/delete leap seconds by slewing instead of stepping.
#leapsecmode slew

# Set the TAI-UTC offset of the system clock.
leapseclist /usr/share/zoneinfo/leap-seconds.list

# Specify directory for log files.
logdir /var/log/chrony

# Select which information is logged.
#log measurements statistics tracking
confdir /etc/chrony.d
~
```

It seems the `makestep` value might need to be adjusted (to `makestep 1.0 -1`)?

> It seems the `makestep` value might need to be adjusted (to `makestep 1.0 -1`)?

This seems right. WDYT? @ninja-quokka @ashley-cui @Luap99 

Good job. @podollb

We already set it though in the image build, is that not being read somehow?

https://github.com/containers/podman-machine-os/blob/77cffc80c2b0e7813b07c48a17b5e9d86a4b11f0/podman-image/build_common.sh#L15-L19
It's interesting. I tried to check if it was correctly loaded, and it seems that it is loaded correctly. Now I am trying to reproduce that.

When the system wakes from sleep, `chronyd` takes about six minutes to synchronize the time. This delay likely occurs because `chronyd` must first verify that the clock is out of sync, and its minimum polling interval for NTP servers is `64 seconds`.

While we could configure a faster polling interval, this approach has a couple of drawbacks. First, it wouldn't solve the problem when the system is without a connection to the internet. Second, sending more frequent requests to public NTP servers would be inconsiderate to their operators.

Another approach is to run `sudo hwclock -s` in the VM to expedite the initial sync after wakeup or regularly, allowing `chronyd` to achieve a more precise synchronization later.

Another option, similar to the solution Docker describes here: https://www.docker.com/blog/addressing-time-drift-in-docker-desktop-for-mac/

Alternatively, this issue could perhaps be handled by the hypervisor, but I'm not certain.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
This is still happening to me multiple times a day.

> While we could configure a faster polling interval, this approach has a couple of drawbacks. First, it wouldn't solve the problem when the system is without a connection to the internet. Second, sending more frequent requests to public NTP servers would be inconsiderate to their operators.

If we don't want to decrease the polling interval, maybe we can use the `timesync` option with vfkit which sends the host time to the qemu-guest-agent service on the guest (how crc does it). On krunkit, a similar option needs to be implemented to maintain parity with vfkit.

> If we don't want to decrease the polling interval, maybe we can use the `timesync` option with vfkit which sends the host time to the qemu-guest-agent service on the guest (how crc does it). On krunkit, a similar option needs to be implemented to maintain parity with vfkit.

That sounds like a possible solution to this issue.  WDYT? @baude  @jakecorrenti

Do we know if this is isolated to a single provider on macOS or if it's present on both?

@ashishshah-ac what provider are you using?

running `podman machine ssh podman-machine-default-2 sudo systemctl restart chronyd` appears to be fixing it but the problem comes back every time.

I have the same issue with the date/time running out of sync between my Mac host and the Linux VM with Podman. 

### Workaround
My **workaround** for the moment is this cronjob:

```
* * * * * /opt/podman/bin/podman machine ssh sudo date -s "'$(/bin/date +\%Y-\%m-\%d\ \%H:\%M:\%S)'" > /dev/null 2>&1
```

### Steps to reproduce
This is from yesterday where my system has been in sleep mode for a few minutes (before I enabled the cronjob):
```
date && podman machine ssh date && podman run alpine date 
Mo 23 Feb 2026 11:43:38 CET
Mon Feb 23 11:28:18 CET 2026
Mon Feb 23 10:28:18 UTC 2026
```

In krunkit @vyasgun just added a `--timesync` (https://github.com/containers/krunkit/pull/89) flag that may fix this. I can do some testing tomorrow to see if it fixes the issue
This issue is solved with https://github.com/podman-container-tools/podman/issues/28345
